title: rome_civilization_The_Aeneid_by_Virgil
paragraphs:
The Trojans, after a seven-year voyage, set sail for Italy, but were overtaken by a dreadful storm raised by Aeolus at Juno's request. Venus, by device, instills in Dido a passion for him. After some conversation, she expresses her desire to hear about his adventures since the siege of Troy, which will be the subject of the next two books. Now scarce was the Trojan fleet, with sails and oars,
Had left behind the fair Sicilian shores. Entering with cheerful shouts the wat'ry reign,
And plowing frothy furrows in the main;
When, laboring still with endless discontent,
The Queen of Heaven did thus her fury vent:
"Then am I vanquished."

Thus while he dealt it round, the pious chief
with cheerful words allayed the common grief:
"Endure, and conquer! What greater ills can you bear? You promised once a divine progeny of Romans,
rising from the Trojan line, in after times to hold the world in awe,
and give the law to land and ocean."

On brazen steps the marble threshold rose,
And cedar beams were encased in brazen plates. The rafters were crowned with brazen coverings;
The lofty doors swung on brazen hinges. For a while, he raised his wondering eyes and gazed around the temple, admiring the fortune of the rising town and the striving artists. He saw in order painted on the wall whatever had befallen unhappy Troy: The wars that fame had blown around the world, all to life, and every leader known. He stopped, and weeping said: "O friend" Even here, the monuments of Trojan woes appear. Even the silent walls proclaim the warrior's fame,
And Trojan sorrows stir the Tyrians' compassion. He said, his tears a ready passage found,
Devouring what he saw so well designed. And with an empty picture fed his mind:
For there he saw the fainting Greeks yield,
And here the trembling Trojans quit the field,
Pursued by fierce Achilles through the plain,
On his high chariot driving o'er the slain.

While the Trojan prince gazes upon the walls,
With wonder and surprise, he beholds
The beautiful Dido, accompanied by a numerous retinue
And the pomp of her guards as she ascends to the sacred shrine. A modest queen, with downcast eyes, pondered the speech. Then she replied briefly: "Trojans, dismiss your fears. My cruel fate and the doubts that attend an unsettled state force me to defend my coast against foreign foes." The clouds parted, and the mist rose.

While the sun shines down upon the earth,
And radiant light illuminates the heavens' birth;
The trees that stand upon the mountain's height
Supply your honor, name, and praise with endless light. Whatever abode my fortune has assigned, your image shall be present in my mind. Thus having said, he turned with pious haste,
And joyful his expecting friends embraced. With his right hand Ilioneus was blessed,
Serestus with his left; then to his breast
Cloanthus and the noble Gyas pressed;
And so by turns they came to the rest. Enter, my noble guest, and you shall find
If not a costly welcome, yet a kind. For I myself, like you, have been distressed,
Till heaven afforded me this place of rest. Like you, an alien in a foreign land,
I learn to pity woes so like my own. "She led her guest to the palace, where she offered incense and proclaimed a feast."

Aeneas recounts how the city of Troy fell, after a ten-year siege, due to the treachery of Sinon and the cleverness of a wooden horse. When he arrives at the designated meeting point, he is met with a large gathering of people. However, he is unable to find his wife. Later, her ghost appears to him, and informs him about the land that was intended for him. In sight of Troy lies Tenedos, an island renowned for its wealth. But now it's a faithless haven, where ships are exposed to the wind and weather.

Some things are designed by fraud or force. Do not trust their gifts, nor accept a horse. Thus, having said, he threw his forceful spear, which hissed as it flew and pierced through the yielding planks of jointed wood. In the hollow belly of the steed, it trembled. The sides, pierced, return a rattling sound,
And groans of Greeks enclosed come issuing through
the wound. Had Heaven not designed the fall of Troy,
or had men not been destined to be blind,
Enough was said and done to inspire a better mind. The miscreant trembled with fear, unarmed and bound. He stared, rolling his haggard eyes about, then spoke in a voice laced with despair: "Alas, woe is me." What of the earth remains, and what sea is open to receive me.

What fate awaits a wretched fugitive, scorned by my enemies and abandoned by my allies. He said, and sighed, casting a rueful glance. Our pity stirs, and our passions fade. We celebrate the young man's right to defend himself,
And freely share with us who he is and where he comes from:
What news he can bring, we eagerly await,
And what truth we can trust from one who has been taken "My fate be what it will, my words shall be true: I am bound to claim my birth; Greece is my homeland, and Sinon is my name." If any chance has brought your name, Palamedes, not unknown to fame,
who suffered from the malice of the times,
accused and sentenced for pretended crimes,
because you sought to prevent these fatal wars. Your death, the wretched Greeks now lament;
I, then a boy, was left with my poor father,
bare of other means, committed to his care. He was your kinsman and companion in war.

While fortune favored me, while my arms supported the cause and ruled the court's counsel, I made some figure there; nor was my name obscure, nor was I without my share of fame. But truly tell, was it for force or guile, or some religious end that you raised the pile? Thus said the king. He, full of fraudulent arts, this well-invented tale imparts: 'Ye lamps of heaven!' he said, and lifted his hands high, 'thou venerable sky!'

I wept to see the visionary man,
And as my trance began, I cried out:
"Oh, light of Troy, and defender of its people,
Your father's greatest warrior, and your nation's pride." After so many years of your own funeral, you are restored to your declining hometown. Troy now commends her future state,
And gives her gods' companions of your fate. From their assistance, walls should be expected,
Which, wandering long, will finally be erected. He said, and brought me from their blessed abodes,
The venerable statues of the gods. With ancient Vesta from the sacred choir,
The wreaths and relics of the immortal fire. “The sounds of shouting echo through the distance,
Cries, threats, and loud lamentations mingle with war. The noise draws near, though our palace remains aloof,
Surrounded by a forest that stands apart from the street.”

Where do we make a stand? What may yet be done?' Scarce had I spoken when Pantheus groaned: 'Troy is no more, and Ilium was a town! When I saw him resolute in arms to fall, and that one spirit animated all. "Brave souls!" I said, "but brave, alas, in vain." Come, finish what our cruel fates ordain. Androgeos fell among us, with his band, who thought us Greeks newly come to land.

'From whence,' he asked, 'my friends, this long delay?'

You arrive after the spoils have been taken: Our ships are already laden with the Trojan treasure. You join them late, like stragglers on shore. He said, but soon corrected his mistake. Found in the doubtful answers we give: Amazingly, he would have avoided the unequal fight. But we, more numerous, intercepted his flight. Coroebus, then, with youthful hopes beguiled,
Swollen with success, and a daring mind,
This new invention was designed to be fatal. "My friends," said he, "since fortune shows the way, it is fitting that we should follow its auspicious guidance."  The input "They find us arms" appears to be a fragment and lacks context. This said, he dressed himself in Androgeus' spoils, his upper vest, his painted shield, and his plume.

While their scattered parties we defeat,
Some flee to shore and safer vessels;
Others, oppressed by baseless dread,
Remount the empty horse, and pant in silence. “But, ah What use of valor can be made when heaven's propitious powers refuse their aid? So hot was the assault, so high did the tumult rise,
While we defended, and while the Greeks opposed us. As all the Dardan and Argolic race had been confined
In that narrow space; or as Ilium itself was void of fear,
And tumult, war, and slaughter, only there. Their targets in a tortoise cast, the foes,
Securely advancing to the turrets rose:
Some mount scaling ladders; others, more bold,
Swerve upwards and by posts and pillars hold. Their left hand grips bucklers as they ascend,
While with their right, they seize the battlement.

From their demolished towers, the Trojans hurl huge heaps of stones, which fall and crush the enemy. They also use heavy beams and rafters from the sides (such arms they resort to in their last need) and gilded roofs that come tumbling down from above, bearing the marks of state and ancient royalty. We renewed our courage with recovered breath,
A second time we rushed to tempt our fate,
To clear the palace of its enemy,
And avenge the lives that had been lost in wait. A postern door, unobserved and free,
Joined by the length of a blind gallery,
To the king's closet led: a way well known
To Hector's wife, while Priam held the throne,
Through which she brought Astyanax, unseen,
To cheer his grandfather and his queen. Beams from its lofty height with swords we shape,
Then, wrenching with our hands, the assault renews;
And, where the rafters on the columns meet,
We push them headlong with our arms and feet.

In our company, you will find a shared refuge. Alternatively, you may choose to share our common destiny. She said, and with her final salute, she embraced the poor old man, who was then placed beneath the laurel. The youth, transfixed by lamentable cries, expires before his wretched parent's eyes. His mother gasps at his feet when Priam sees him. The fear of death gives way to nature's law; and shaking more with anger than with age, he says, "The gods, requite your brutal rage." He comforted my sorrows, and for a sum of gold, he sold the lifeless body of my Hector; pitied the hardships a parent endures, and sent me safely back to his tent. "This said, his feeble hand threw a javelin, which fluttered and seemed to linger as it flew. It just barely reached its mark and faintly struck the bronze shield." "Then Pyrrhus said: 'Go, you to your fate. Tell my father of my wicked deeds.'"

She shall review her kingdom and her friends,
In state attended by a captive crew,
While unavenged, the good old Priam falls,
And Greek fires consume the Trojan walls. Cast your eyes around, as I dispel
The misty veil that shrouds mortal sight. Clear away the impurities, and behold
The form of each vengeful deity. Haste, my son; put an end to this fruitless labor. Hurry, for your wife and father are waiting: Hurry, and I will ensure your journey is accompanied by a mother's love. She said, and vanished into the darkness.

Restore me to the unfinished fight. My death is waiting to conclude the night. It doesn't contain grammatical errors in the classical sense but rather employs poetic license. However, if you'd like, I can attempt to modernize the text while preserving its original meaning:

Armed once again, my shining sword I wield,
While the other hand supports my heavy shield. I rush forth to seek the abandoned field. I went; but sad Creusa stopped my way. She crossed the threshold in my path and embraced my knees. When I tried to leave, she showed me my weak father and tender son: 'If death is your intention,' she said, 'at least take us with you to share your fate.' To whom should I reveal my own life, as well as that of my son and yours, who is now long gone. While thus she fills the house with clamorous cries, Our hearing is diverted by our eyes: For, while I held my son, in the short space between our kisses and our last embrace; Strange to relate, from young Iulus' head a lambent flame arose, which gently spread around his brows, and on his temples fed.

It swept a path in heaven, and shone as a guide. The good old man with supplicant hands implored the gods' protection, and his star was adored. "My son," he said, "there is no more time for delay."  "He said." Our country's gods, relics, and bands hold you, my father, in your guiltless hands. It is impious to bear holy things, red as I am with slaughter, new from war, until I cleanse the guilt of dire debate and spilled blood in battle. Thus, ordering all that prudence could provide,
I clothe my shoulders with a lion's hide and yellow spoils. Then, on my bending back, I take the welcome load
Of my dear father; while on my better hand Ascanius hangs,
And with unequal paces trips along.

My father, looking through the shadows, with fear, cried out: "Haste, haste, my son, the enemies are near; their swords and shining armor I see." Some hostile god, for some unknown offense,
Had taken away my better judgment;
For, while I wandered through life's troubled paths,
I sought solace in the darkness of the night. When many painful years have passed, you will find yourself on the happy shores of Latium,
Where the gentle Tiber river flows from its bed,
And behold the lush meadows where the cattle graze. The parent of the gods restrains my fleeting soul in her abode. I entrust our common heritage to your care. She said, and passed silently through the air.

My sire, my son, our lesser and greater gods,
All sail at once, and cleave the briny deep. Against our coast appears a spacious land,
Which once the fierce Lycurgus did command. Thracia, the name; the people bold in war;
Their fields are vast, and tillage is their care. A hospitable realm while fate was kind,
With Troy in friendship and religion aligned. While I was cropping the forest scenes,
And shading our altar with their leafy greens,
I pulled up a plant; with horror I recount
A wonder so strange and full of destiny. Let not my prayers be met with doubt; but show me clearly your will. He shook the holy ground, the laurels, and the lofty hills around;
And from the tripos rushed a bellowing sound.

If Jupiter assists the passage of our fleet, the third propitious dawn discovers Crete. Thus having said, the sacrifices he made were laid on smoking altars. He offered a bull to Neptune, as was his due, and another bull to Apollo, who shone brightly. A milk-white ewe was given to the western winds to please them, while a coal-black one was sacrificed to calm the stormy seas. He found his mistake in a small space; it was not derived from Crete, nor was he deceived by the uncertain seat. "O son," he said, "tormented by your Trojan fate." Let us follow Phoebus. "He said, and we with glad consent obeyed. Forsaking the seat, we left a few behind and spread our sails before the willing wind."

Heed my words; and remember what Jupiter decrees, what Phoebus has ordained. As the queen of the Furies, I warn you that the Italian shores are foredoomed by fate. You will find them granted to you, and a safe passage to the assigned port will be yours. Fierce famine is your fate for this misdeed; reduced to grinding the plates on which you feed. She said, and to the neighboring forest flew. But old Anchises, offering sacrifice,
Lifted his hands and eyes to heaven. He adored the greater gods:
"Avert these omens," he said.
"Render this prophecy vain,
And free a pious people from the coming curse." Thus having said, he bids us set sail. We release our anchors from the shore and obey his command. Soon we follow the winding path of the sea with our swelling sails.

We resolved to rest awhile from labor's past,
Our crooked anchors cast from the prow at last,
And hastened joyfully to the little city. But when she was near enough to see my shining armor and Trojan shield, she was astonished by the sight. Her vital heat left her limbs; her veins no longer beat. She faints, falls, and barely recovers her strength. With a faltering tongue, she speaks at length: "Are you alive, O goddess-born one?" "She said, 'If it's a ghost, then where is Hector's shade?' She let out a loud and frightful cry. You're Hector's son. Or is Hector fled? His memory lost in Pyrrhus' bed. With eyes cast down, in a subdued tone, she began: "Oh, only happy maid of Priam's line, Who was spared by death from the enemy's grasp."

First, then, that happy shore seems so near,
Yet it will far from your deluded wishes flee. Long stretches of sea divide your hopes from Italy:
You must sail along Sicilian shores and stem the currents with your struggling oars. Then steer your navy along the Italian coast;
After this, veer towards Circe's island. Finally, before your new foundations rise,
You must pass the Stygian lake and view the underworld skies. A human face, and a virgin bosom, hide her shame. It is safer to sail away from the sea and along the coast of Pachynus, though at a slower pace, than to approach the distorted form of Scylla and listen to the loud howling of the water wolves. If faith to Helenus be due,
And if prophetic Phoebus tells me true,
Do not this precept of your friend forget,
Which therefore more than once I must repeat:
Above all, adore great Juno's name;
Pay vows to Juno; seek her aid. Thus, many failed, and most condemned
The madness of the visionary maid,
And with loud curses left the mystic shade. Do not think it a waste of time to stay awhile,
Though your companions scold your prolonged delay;
Though summoned to the seas, though pleasant winds
Invite you on your course, and swell your sails:
But ask the sacred priestess to tell with willing words,
And not to write your fate.

It seems We saw the giant shepherd stalk before us on the mountain's brow. His following flock was led by him, and he guided them towards the shore. The shepherd was a monstrous bulk, deformed and blind. He carried a staff made from a pine trunk to help him navigate. After endless toil, I lost you, dear father,
Spent with age, tossed by raging storms on every coast. I have lost ease from my cares and solace from my pain,
Despite having endured a thousand trials, in vain. The prophet who revealed my future woes remained silent;
Yet this, the greatest and worst of all, was concealed. Even Celaeno, whose foreboding skill had warned
All else, was silent about the ill that befell.

No He who has my vows shall ever have. For he whom I loved on earth is now worshipped in heaven. "She cried, and tears streamed down her face, silencing her words." Dearer than the vital air I breathe,
Will you to grief your blooming years bestow? Condemned to waste in woes your lonely life,
Without the joys of mother, wife, or child. The text appears to be a poem, possibly from Shakespeare's "Antony and Cleopatra". A milk-white heifer she adorns with flowers,
And pours the ruddy wine between her horns. And while the priests pray to the gods,
She feeds their altars with Sabaean smoke,
With hourly care the sacrifice renews,
And anxiously views the panting entrails.

You are a partner in both his bed and throne. Your influence can shape his thoughts; my desires are yours. "My care," said Imperial Juno, "be yours. Time presses on, and now is the moment to complete this matter. Heed my counsel, and you shall be privy to the secret." While the huntsmen pitch their toils around,
Cheerful horns resound from side to side. A dark cloud shall cover all the plain
With hail, thunder, and tempestuous rain;
The fearful train shall take its speedy flight,
Dispersed, and all involved in gloomy night. One cave shall offer a grateful shelter
To the fair princess and the Trojan lord. He was resolute on the course, but uncertain of the method. After much deliberation, he decided to follow this counsel: Three chiefs were summoned, instructed to repair the fleet and ship their men with quiet diligence; He tasked them with finding a plausible pretext to justify his true intentions.

He remained unmoved, his eyes fixed by Jove's command. He did not allow love to stir within him, despite the turmoil in his heart. Then, at length, he replied: "Fair queen, your kindness knows no bounds. I am forever in your debt. My mind cannot forget Eliza's name, even as my mortal breath sustains me." Fair queen, do not oppose the will of the gods. I am forced by fate to leave your happy land. Thus, while he spoke, she began to view
the guilty man with sparkling eyes. From head to foot, she surveyed his person,
and no longer did he dare utter such threats.
"False as you are," he said, "and more than false, you have sworn." Now, sinking beneath a load of grief,
From death alone she seeks her final relief. The time and means are resolved within her breast;
She turns to her mournful sister thus:

"Rejoice not for me," she says with sorrow's face,
"For I have found my end, my final place."
Her cloudy front clears, and a false vigour appears,
A fleeting calm that hides her deep despair. She said. "My lover, instructed from above, I shall either gain or lose my love."

Next, beneath this, the bridal bed should be placed. It was there that I found my ruin in his arms embraced. All remnants of him were doomed to destruction by fire. This was as required by the priestess and her charms. Thus far she had spoken, and further speech was forborne. A mortal pallor appeared on her face: Yet the mistrustful Anna could not discern The secret funeral rites designed; Nor did she think such dire passion possessed her mind. She feared no worse than when Sichaeus fell. She thought to herself, "What should I do?"

Haste swiftly hence, while you have power to fly. Prevent her rage, while night obscures the skies,
And sail before the dawn breaks forth in purple hue. Lead you the way; protect your Trojan forces,
And prosper the design that your will commands. He said: and drawing forth his flaming sword,
His thundering arm cuts through the many-twisted cord.

She turned to Sichaeus' nurse and said briefly, "Go, Barce, call my sister." But when she viewed the garments loosely spread,
Which once he wore, and saw the conscious bed,
She paused, and with a sigh, the robes embraced;
Then on the couch, her trembling body cast,
Repressed the ready tears, and spoke her last:
"Dear pledges of my love, while heaven pleased,
Receive a soul, of mortal anguish eased:
My fatal course is finished; I go,
A glorious name, among the ghosts below." Could my fortune have been spared if the Trojan had not touched my land? Then kissed the couch; and, "Must I die," she said, "and be unrevened?" It is twice as good to die.

These flames, from afar, foretell a false Trojan view. "She struck, and a spear plunged deep into her side. The blade was smeared with blood, its hilt stained purple. The sword remained lodged in the wound, while blood streamed from it onto her hands." "All this was just a clever plot," she said, "all designed to deceive an unhappy me." Bring water. Bathe the wound. While I lay dying, she clutched me tightly and caught her last breath. This said, she mounted the pile with eager haste,
And in her arms, the gasping queen was embraced;
Her temples were chafed, and her own garments rent,
To stem the flowing blood and clean the wound.

The goddess descended and took flight, drawing a thousand colors from the light. She stood above the dying lover's head and said, "I dedicate you to the dead." This offering to the infernal gods I bring. Thus, as she spoke, she cut her fatal hair. Juno sends Iris to persuade the Trojan woman to burn the ships. The woman, upon Iris' instigation, sets fire to them. Four of the ships were burned, but they would have been consumed if Jupiter hadn't intervened with a miraculous storm that put out the flames.

Then to his fearless chief he said, "Not Heaven," though Jupiter himself promised Italy could stem the torrent of this raging sea. But now assist the rites with garlands crowned. He said and first bound his brows with myrtle. The next Centaur and Dolphin's crew renewed their vanished hopes of victory in the race to reach the mark.

Sergesthus takes his place; Mnestheus pursues. As they sail around, he comes up, not half a ship's length behind. On the deck, among his comrades, he appears. He then lifts their drooping spirits: "My friends and Hector's followers, exert your strength! Pull the oar with vigor; stretch out to your strokes, my unconquerable crew. I drew you from the flames of Troy." ” He said The next, but though the next, yet far apart, came Salius and Euryalus behind. Then Helymus, whom young Diores pursued step by step, his shoulders almost pressed against hers, had won or left at least a doubtful race.

Fortune's mistakes allow me to make amends,
At least to show compassion for a worthy friend. He said, and from among the spoils, he drew a lion's hide. To Salius, it was given. Nisus weeps with envy at the sight of the gift. "If such rewards are due to vanquished men," "He said, 'And in falling, there's a chance to rise again. What reward can Nisus claim from your generosity? Who deserves the first honors and praise?' Could I turn to that fair prime once more,
That prime of which this boastful one is so vain,
The brave warrior who in this decrepit age
Defies his fate and feels my force, unguaranteed. He said; and, rising at the word, he threw two ponderous gauntlets down in open view. Gauntlets that Eryx would not in a fight wield, and sheathed his hands within the listed field.

Amazed by their weight, the hero stood tall. "What had been your wonder," said Entellus, "Had you seen the gauntlets of Alcides or viewed the stern debate on this unhappy green." With this arm, I sustained the Herculean form;
And with this hand, I wielded it while my blood was still warm. This body languished, as better spirits took its place,
Until age had loosened my nerves, and time had dulled my mind. If great Aeneas and Acestes join in his request,
I'll wield their weight and use my arms. Let's fight with equal strength,
And let him leave to fear. Entellus prepares for the strife, stripped of his quilted coat, his body is exposed. Composed of mighty bones and muscle, he towers over the sand.

His hand and watchful eye keep even pace; 
While Dares traverses and shifts his place,
And, like a captain who besieges round
Some strong-built castle on a rising ground,
Views all the approaches with observing eyes:
This and that other part in vain he tries,
And more on industry than force relies. The gods," said he, "To him propitious, and averse to you, have given their arm superior strength to yours. It is folly to resist divine power. The gauntlet fight thus ended,
From the shore, his faithful friends did bear
Their unhappy Dares. His mouth and nostrils poured
A purple flood; his pounding teeth came rushing with his blood. The Dardan prince donned a smiling face,
And tightly clasped Acestes in a close embrace;
Then, honoring him with gifts beyond the rest,
He turned the bad omen and did not confess fear. "The gods," he said, "have wrought this miracle. They ordered your prize."

Accept this goblet, adorned with figured gold, which the Thracian Cissus gave to my father of old. Receive this token of ancient friendship, which I now bestow on you. He was proclaimed victor, and with the trumpets' cheerful sound, he was crowned with a laurel wreath. The chief, before the games were wholly done, called Periphantes, tutor to his son. He whispered thus: "With speed, Ascanius find; and if his childish troop is ready, join him on horseback to grace his grandfather's day, and lead his equals armed in just array." He said; and calling out, "The circus clears." The first young Priam led,
A lovely boy, whose grand sire was the unhappy King of Troy. His lineage in later times was renowned for fame,
New honors added to the Latin name;
And well the royal youth became skilled on his Thracian steed.

Beroe but now I left, pained by pain, her age and anguish from these rites detain," she said. Eumelus was the first to bear the news, while yet they crowded the rural theatre. Ascanius took alarm, leading his early warriors on his prancing steed, and spurred them on; his equals soon overpassed him. Nor could his frightened friends reclaim his haste.

A common town for all. From Acestes' name, it is called Acesta. The reasons, combined with his friend's experience, encouraged him greatly, but more disturbed his mind. It was dead of night; when to his slumbering eyes His father's shade descended from the skies. And thus he spoke: "O more vital breath, loved while I lived, and dear even after death. O son, in various toils and troubles tossed, The King of Heaven employs my careful ghost on his commands. The god who saved your flaming fleet and heard your just desire." I fade into the darkness, and I am enveloped by heaven's approaching dawn. He said, and mixed with shadows, then took to the air. Please provide the entire text you would like me to correct, and  "And why, oh why, was the longed-for embrace denied?" "He rose, inspired by holy zeal. Like a blacksmith tending his forge, he tends the fiery coals, renewing the flames. His people's gods and Vesta then offer him cakes and incense, seeking his aid." Should I trust the Sirens of old,
And often deceived, not recognize my own fears. "He said: His fastened hands held the rudder tight, and fixed on heaven, his eyes repelled the invasion of sleep."

The victor daemon mounts in air,
While the ship sails without the pilot's care. He said, and wept; then spread his sails before
The winds, and reached at length the Cumaean shore:
Their anchors dropped, his crew moored the vessels. Thus, while their several charges they fulfilled,
The pious prince ascends the sacred hill,
Where Phoebus is adored;
and seeks the shade that hides from sight
his venerable maid.

Aeneas viewed all this with wondering eyes. Each varying object renewed his delight. Eager to read on, Achates arrived, accompanied by the mad divining priestess, Deiphobe. "Time does not suffer," she said, "to be wasted on empty pleasures. Haste makes waste." Seven bulls, untouched, for Phoebus chose,
And for Diana seven unspotted ewes. This said, the servants urged the sacred rites. The prince invited her to the temple. He has come; behold the God. Thus, as she spoke, her color changed; her face was no longer the same. Hollow groans echoed from her troubled spirit.

Your prayers alone can open this abode; otherwise, my requests are in vain, and the god is silent. She said no more. Not less than theirs from Jupiter, my lineage came; my mother was greater, and our descent was the same. So I prayed, and while I prayed, I placed my hand on the holy altar. Besides, you do not know, while you are here, the unhappy fate of your friend: he lies breathless, and his unburied ghost pollutes this place without funeral rites.

Pay first your pious dues; and, for the dead,
Two black sheep are led around his hearse. Then, living turf is laid upon his body:
This done, securely take the destined way,
To find the regions bereft of day. She said, and held her peace. As he worked, he pondered the ways to achieve his desire. He gazed into the gloomy forest and made a vow to the Queen of Love: "May your power, always favorable to me, guide my steps to find the fatal tree in this dark wood. The Sibyl's prophecy foretold it, alas." Misenus' death was too true. Two doves descended from their aerial flight and landed securely on the grassy plain. Thou, great parent, with celestial care,
Be present in my prayer. Thus having said, he stopped with watchful sight,

Astonished Aunus just arrives by chance,
To see his fall; nor does he dare advance further. But, fixing on the horrid maid, he stares,
And shakes with fear, finding it futile to flee. Yet, like a true Ligurian, born to deceive,
He cries out aloud: "What courage have you shown? Who trust your courser's strength and not your own?" Forgo the advantage of your horse, alight,
And then on equal terms begin the fight:
It shall be seen, weak woman, what you can,
When, foot to foot, you combat with a man. These are your studied cares,
Your lewd delight is swift to debauch,
But slow to manly fight. He spurred forward among his enemies, not managing to save the life he had intended.

And in arms be known, for Hector's nephew and Aeneas' son. He strode out onto the plain. Menoetes lived a peaceful life after killing the men of Lerna, who had long avoided the dangers of battle. He spent his days on the lake's shore, fishing with his nets and line to earn his living. Unlike those who sought wealth and status, Menoetes was content with simplicity; he shunned the trappings of power and luxury. His humble abode was modest, but it was enough for him. His father had struggled to provide for him, and as a result, Menoetes' rent was paid in full by his father's labor on another man's land.

A man stood frozen with fear as he gazed at his shield, his eyes fixed on the spear hurtling towards him. Suddenly, a surge of rage coursed through him, and he bellowed, "Traitor! You claim to be worthy of my friend's honor, yet you're adorned with trophies that were won by their sacrifice."
